schools.summary: Sarai's barrenness and Hagar: 'now Sarai Abram's wife bare him no children: and she had an handmaid, an Egyptian, whose name was Hagar' — Sarai remaining barren (11:30), childless after ten years in Canaan (v.3, the long delay of the promised seed, the trial of faith that pressed on Abram and Sarai, the human impossibility against which God's promise stood); Sarai having 'an handmaid, an Egyptian, whose name was Hagar' (Hagar, an Egyptian servant, perhaps acquired during the sojourn in Egypt, 12:16, the maid whom Sarai would give to Abram, v.2, the occasion of the trouble that follows); Sarai's barrenness and the presence of an Egyptian maid setting the stage for the expedient (v.2, Sarai's plan to obtain children by Hagar, the impatience of waiting for the promise, the setting for the birth of Ishmael and the long trial before Isaac) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Henry.

themes: Now Sarai Abram's wife bare him no children: and she had an handmaid, an Egyptian, whose name was Hagar
Sarai's barrenness and her Egyptian handmaid Hagar
the long delay of the promised seed, the trial of faith
the setting for the expedient and the birth of Ishmael

schools.summary: Calvin, Gill, and many (a fault of unbelief): 'and Sarai said unto Abram, Behold now, the LORD hath restrained me from bearing: I pray thee, go in unto my maid; it may be that I may obtain children by her. And Abram hearkened to the voice of Sarai' — Sarai acknowledging that the LORD had withheld children from her, yet, instead of waiting in faith for God to give the seed, devising a plan to obtain children through her maid (her reasoning mixing a recognition of God's hand with an impatient expedient); the act a fault, a failure of faith and patience (Sarai, weary of waiting for the promise, 12:2; 15:4, taking matters into her own hands, giving Hagar to Abram to obtain a child by her, the child reckoned as Sarai's, by the custom of the time, the expedient springing from unbelief and impatience, not waiting on God's way and time, leading to strife and trouble, vv.4-6); 'Abram hearkened to the voice of Sarai' (Abram yielding to Sarai's proposal, as Adam to Eve, 3:17, his compliance too a failure of faith, taking the seed by human contrivance rather than waiting for God, the patriarch, with Sarai, leaning on his own way) — Calvin, Gill, Henry.

The custom-reading (mitigating the act): the giving of a handmaid to bear children for a barren wife a recognized custom of the age (later seen in Rachel and Leah, ch. 30), Sarai acting by the custom of her time, with good intent (to obtain the promised seed), the polygamy, though against the original institution (2:24), countenanced by the custom and not yet plainly forbidden; on this reading Sarai's act is excused or mitigated as a lawful expedient of the age, though it sprang from a want of faith to wait on God's promise and brought trouble (vv.4-6). The act divides between a fault of unbelief/impatience and an excusable custom of the age.

debates.question: Was Sarai's giving Hagar to Abram a sin or an excusable custom?

sides.position: A fault of unbelief and impatience — not waiting on God's promise of a son, taking the seed by human contrivance, leading to strife
An excusable expedient by the custom of the age — the giving of a handmaid for a barren wife, with good intent

themes: And Sarai said unto Abram... I pray thee, go in unto my maid; it may be that I may obtain children by her. And Abram hearkened to the voice of Sarai
Sarai's expedient of giving Hagar to Abram (the crux of the act)
a fault of unbelief and impatience vs an excusable custom of the age
the seed sought by human contrivance, Abram hearkening to Sarai

schools.summary: Sarai gives Hagar as wife: 'and Sarai Abram's wife took Hagar her maid the Egyptian, after Abram had dwelt ten years in the land of Canaan, and gave her to her husband Abram to be his wife' — Sarai formally giving Hagar to Abram as a (secondary) wife (the maid raised to the place of a wife, to bear the desired seed, the expedient, v.2, carried out); 'after Abram had dwelt ten years in the land of Canaan' (ten years having passed since the promise, 12:2, and the entry into Canaan, yet no child, the long delay that wearied Sarai's patience and prompted the expedient, the trial of faith through the years of waiting); 'to be his wife' (Hagar becoming Abram's wife, a concubine, a secondary wife, the polygamy that, though by the custom of the age, was against the original institution, 2:24, the union that would bring strife, vv.4-6, and the birth of Ishmael, the human contrivance for the seed) — Gill, Ellicott, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: And Sarai Abram's wife took Hagar her maid the Egyptian, after Abram had dwelt ten years in the land of Canaan, and gave her to her husband Abram to be his wife
Sarai giving Hagar to Abram as wife after ten years in Canaan
the long delay of the promise that prompted the expedient
the polygamy of the expedient, against the original institution (2:24)

schools.summary: Hagar conceives and despises Sarai: 'and he went in unto Hagar, and she conceived: and when she saw that she had conceived, her mistress was despised in her eyes' — Abram taking Hagar, and she conceiving (the maid, who had been the barren mistress's hope, now with child, the conception that would breed pride in Hagar and strife in the household); 'when she saw that she had conceived, her mistress was despised in her eyes' (Hagar, finding herself with child, which Sarai was not, growing proud and despising her barren mistress, the maid lifted up against the wife, the pride and insolence of Hagar, the bitter fruit of the expedient — the very plan to help bringing contempt and strife); Hagar's contempt of Sarai the first bitter fruit of the polygamous expedient (v.2, the strife and jealousy that polygamy breeds, Sarai, who had given Hagar, now suffering her scorn, the household disturbed, the consequence of leaning on human contrivance rather than God's promise) — Ellicott, Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: And he went in unto Hagar, and she conceived: and when she saw that she had conceived, her mistress was despised in her eyes
Hagar's conceiving and her despising of Sarai
the maid grown proud against the barren wife
the strife and contempt the polygamous expedient bred in the household

schools.summary: Sarai complains to Abram: 'and Sarai said unto Abram, My wrong be upon thee: I have given my maid into thy bosom; and when she saw that she had conceived, I was despised in her eyes: the LORD judge between me and thee' — Sarai complaining bitterly to Abram, laying the blame (the wrong, the injury she suffered from Hagar's contempt) upon him (the jealousy and grief of the despised wife, Sarai, who had devised the expedient, v.2, now turning the blame on Abram); Sarai recounting her grievance — 'I have given my maid into thy bosom; and when she saw that she had conceived, I was despised in her eyes' (she having given Hagar, and now suffering the maid's scorn, the bitter fruit of her own plan turned against her, the passion and inconsistency of Sarai, blaming Abram for what she herself had contrived); 'the LORD judge between me and thee' (Sarai appealing to God to judge between her and Abram, the heat of her complaint, the strife within the household, brought before God, the trouble that the expedient had bred, now disturbing the peace of Abram and Sarai) — Gill, Ellicott, Calvin.

themes: And Sarai said unto Abram, My wrong be upon thee... the LORD judge between me and thee
Sarai's complaint to Abram over Hagar's contempt
the despised wife blaming Abram for the fruit of her own plan
the household troubled by the strife the expedient bred

schools.summary: Abram yields Hagar, Hagar flees: 'but Abram said unto Sarai, Behold, thy maid is in thy hand; do to her as it pleaseth thee. And when Sarai dealt hardly with her, she fled from her face' — Abram, to keep peace, giving Sarai authority over Hagar (the maid still being Sarai's servant, he leaving Hagar to Sarai's disposal, the patriarch's wish to avoid strife, yielding the matter to his wife); 'Sarai dealt hardly with her (afflicted, humbled her)' (Sarai, given the power, treating Hagar harshly, perhaps with excessive severity, in the heat of her jealousy, the rough usage of the proud maid, Sarai's hard dealing, though provoked, not without fault, the affliction that drove Hagar to flee); 'she fled from her face' (Hagar, unable to bear Sarai's harsh treatment, fleeing from her mistress, the proud maid now a fugitive, the flight into the wilderness, v.7, where the angel of the LORD would find her, the trouble of the household driving Hagar away) — Gill, Clarke, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: But Abram said unto Sarai, Behold, thy maid is in thy hand... And when Sarai dealt hardly with her, she fled from her face
Abram yielding Hagar to Sarai and Hagar's flight
the patriarch yielding to keep peace, Sarai's harsh treatment
the proud maid driven to flee into the wilderness

schools.summary: Gill and the dominant Christian reading (the Angel a divine Person): 'and the angel of the LORD found her by a fountain of water in the wilderness, by the fountain in the way to Shur' — 'the angel of the LORD' here not a created angel but a divine Person, the uncreated Angel, the Logos or Son of God (a pre-incarnate appearance of Christ): the Angel speaking as God ('I will multiply thy seed,' v.10, which only God can do), called and worshiped as Jehovah by Hagar ('Thou God seest me,' v.13), and accepting the name, had he been a created angel he would have refused the divine honor (as Revelation 19:10; 22:9), the dominant view seeing here a theophany, the Angel of the LORD identified with the LORD himself; a second reading (the modern/critical, and some) taking the angel of the LORD as a created angel who speaks and acts as God's representative, in God's name (the messenger bearing the divine word and authority); the Angel finding Hagar 'by a fountain of water in the wilderness, by the fountain in the way to Shur' (the fugitive Hagar, fleeing toward Egypt, Shur, on the way to Egypt, found by the Angel at a fountain in the desert, the grace of God seeking out the afflicted, wandering maid, the LORD's care for Hagar in her distress) — Gill, Henry, Clarke.

debates.question: Who is 'the angel of the LORD'?

sides.position: A divine Person — the uncreated Angel, the Logos/Son of God (a pre-incarnate Christ / theophany), since he speaks as God and is called Jehovah (the dominant Christian reading)
A created angel speaking and acting in God's name, as his representative bearing the divine word

themes: And the angel of the LORD found her by a fountain of water in the wilderness, by the fountain in the way to Shur
the angel of the LORD finding Hagar (the crux of the angel's identity)
a divine Person, the uncreated Angel, the Son of God vs a created angel in God's name
God's grace seeking out the afflicted, wandering maid

schools.summary: The Angel questions Hagar: 'and he said, Hagar, Sarai's maid, whence camest thou? and whither wilt thou go? And she said, I flee from the face of my mistress Sarai' — the Angel addressing Hagar by name and by her station ('Sarai's maid,' recalling her duty), and asking where she came from and where she was going (the question not for information, the Angel knew, but to bring Hagar to reflection — to own her situation and consider her course, the gracious dealing that leads the wanderer to self-examination); the Angel calling her 'Sarai's maid' (reminding Hagar of her place and relation, still Sarai's servant, the title that recalled her duty and her flight from it, the gentle reproof in the address); 'I flee from the face of my mistress Sarai' (Hagar confessing she was fleeing from Sarai, owning her flight, though she could not say whither she was going, having no sure refuge, the honest acknowledgment drawn out by the Angel's question, the fugitive brought to confess her case, preparing for the Angel's counsel, v.9) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: And he said, Hagar, Sarai's maid, whence camest thou? and whither wilt thou go? And she said, I flee from the face of my mistress Sarai
the Angel's questioning of Hagar
the question bringing the fugitive to reflection and confession
the reminder of her place preparing for the Angel's counsel

schools.summary: The Angel bids Hagar return and submit: 'and the angel of the LORD said unto her, Return to thy mistress, and submit thyself under her hands' — the Angel commanding Hagar to return to Sarai and submit (humble herself) under her authority (not to flee her duty, but to go back and bear her lot patiently, the counsel of God to the fugitive — return and submit); Hagar to return to her mistress (the Angel sending her back to her place and duty, the flight from affliction not the way, God would have her return and endure); 'submit thyself under her hands' (Hagar to humble herself and submit to Sarai's authority, her pride, v.4, having helped breed the strife, the counsel of humility and patience, the servant to bear her lot under her mistress, the duty of submission, with the promise, v.10, to encourage her, God's way for Hagar — not flight, but humble return and submission) — Gill, Ellicott, Wesley.

themes: And the angel of the LORD said unto her, Return to thy mistress, and submit thyself under her hands
the Angel's command that Hagar return and submit
not flight from affliction, but humble return and submission
the way of patience, with the promise to encourage her

schools.summary: The Angel promises to multiply Hagar's seed: 'and the angel of the LORD said unto her, I will multiply thy seed exceedingly, that it shall not be numbered for multitude' — the Angel promising to multiply Hagar's seed (through Ishmael), notably, the Angel speaking as God ('I will multiply,' a promise only God can make and fulfill — evidence, with v.13, that the Angel is a divine Person, the promise of a numerous posterity to the afflicted maid); the Angel's saying 'I will multiply thy seed' — taking to himself the divine work — confirming that he is the LORD himself (the uncreated Angel, v.7, the promise spoken in God's own person, the divine assurance to Hagar); 'that it shall not be numbered for multitude' (Hagar's seed, the Ishmaelites, the Arab peoples, innumerable, the promise of a great multitude of descendants, fulfilled in the many Arab nations, 25:12-18; 17:20, twelve princes, the comfort and encouragement given to Hagar to return, v.9, the blessing on the afflicted maid and her son) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And the angel of the LORD said unto her, I will multiply thy seed exceedingly, that it shall not be numbered for multitude
the Angel's promise to multiply Hagar's seed
the Angel speaking as God, confirming he is the LORD himself
the innumerable Ishmaelite/Arab peoples (17:20; 25:12-18)

schools.summary: The naming of Ishmael: 'and the angel of the LORD said unto her, Behold, thou art with child, and shalt bear a son, and shalt call his name Ishmael; because the LORD hath heard thy affliction' — the Angel announcing that Hagar would bear a son and should name him Ishmael (the foretelling of the birth and the giving of the name, the comfort to the afflicted maid); 'Ishmael' meaning 'God shall hear' (or 'God hears,' the name commemorating God's hearing of Hagar's affliction, the son named for the mercy of God to his mother, the meaning of the name a witness to God's regard); 'because the LORD hath heard thy affliction' (the name Ishmael, 'God hears,' given because the LORD had heard, regarded, Hagar's affliction, God's gracious notice of the suffering maid, the comfort that God hears the afflicted and the cry of the distressed, the LORD's mercy to Hagar in her trouble) — Gill, Ellicott, Clarke, Calvin.

themes: And the angel of the LORD said unto her... shalt call his name Ishmael; because the LORD hath heard thy affliction
the naming of Ishmael, 'God hears'
the son named for God's hearing of Hagar's affliction
God's gracious notice of the suffering maid

schools.summary: The character of Ishmael: 'and he will be a wild man; his hand will be against every man, and every man's hand against him; and he shall dwell in the presence of all his brethren' — Ishmael 'a wild man (a wild-ass man, pere adam)' (like the wild ass, Job 39:5-8, free, untamed, roving the desert, the description of a wild, free, nomadic people, the character of Ishmael and his descendants, the Bedouin Arabs, wild and independent); 'his hand will be against every man, and every man's hand against him' (Ishmael's posterity a warlike, contentious people, at strife with their neighbors and resisted by them, the marauding, plundering life of the desert peoples, the perpetual conflict of the Ishmaelite/Arab tribes, the prophecy fulfilled in the history of the Arabs); 'he shall dwell in the presence (in the face, or to the east) of all his brethren' (Ishmael dwelling near, yet independent of, his brethren, the other descendants of Abraham, Isaac's line and Keturah's, the Ishmaelites maintaining their place and independence among the nations, the wild, free people dwelling in the sight of, but distinct from, their kindred, the prophecy of the enduring, independent Arab peoples) — Gill, Clarke, Ellicott, Calvin.

themes: And he will be a wild man; his hand will be against every man, and every man's hand against him; and he shall dwell in the presence of all his brethren
the prophecy of Ishmael's wild and independent character
the free, untamed, warlike posterity of Ishmael (the Arabs)
dwelling near yet independent of his brethren, fulfilled in their history

schools.summary: Hagar names God El-roi: 'and she called the name of the LORD that spake unto her, Thou God seest me: for she said, Have I also here looked after him that seeth me?' — Hagar naming the LORD (whom she recognized in the Angel, v.7) 'El-roi' (the God who sees me, the God of seeing, she confessing that God had seen and regarded her in her affliction, the afflicted maid's confession of the all-seeing God who beheld her in the wilderness, notably, Hagar calling the Angel 'the LORD' and 'God,' confirming the Angel's divine identity, v.7); 'Thou God seest me' (Hagar finding that the eye of God was upon her, seeing her affliction and her flight, the comfort that God sees the afflicted, the name confessing God's gracious, watchful regard, the wonder that God had seen and sought her); 'Have I also here looked after him that seeth me?' the Hebrew difficult and variously rendered (the sense taken either as Hagar's wonder that she had here seen, the back of, God, the Angel, and yet lived, as men feared to see God and die, 32:30; Exodus 33:20, or as her marveling that she had looked after, sought, regarded, the God who sees her, or that even here, in the wilderness, she had seen the God who saw her, the difficult clause expressing Hagar's awe at the divine appearance and the seeing God) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Clarke.

debates.question: How is the difficult clause 'Have I also here looked after him that seeth me?' to be read?

sides.position: Hagar's wonder that she had here seen God (the Angel) and yet lived (cf. 32:30; Exodus 33:20, men feared to see God and die)
Hagar marveling at, or having sought/regarded, the God who sees her, whom she had here beheld in her affliction

themes: And she called the name of the LORD that spake unto her, Thou God seest me: for she said, Have I also here looked after him that seeth me?
Hagar's naming of God, 'Thou God seest me' (the El-roi crux)
the afflicted maid confessing the all-seeing God who beheld her
the difficult clause - wonder at seeing God and living, or at the God who sees her

schools.summary: The well Beer-lahai-roi: 'wherefore the well was called Beerlahairoi; behold, it is between Kadesh and Bered' — the well by which the Angel found Hagar (v.7) named Beer-lahai-roi (the well of the living one who sees me, or the well of him that liveth and seeth me, commemorating the appearance of the living God who saw and spoke to her, the name a memorial of God's gracious meeting with Hagar, the well located between Kadesh and Bered, in the wilderness toward Shur); the name 'Beer-lahai-roi' (variously rendered, 'the well of the living one that seeth me,' or 'the well of him that liveth and seeth me') commemorating Hagar's confession (v.13) that the living God had seen her (the well a standing memorial of God's mercy to the afflicted maid, the place named for the divine encounter); 'between Kadesh and Bered' (the well lying in the wilderness, between Kadesh and Bered, places in the south, toward Shur and Egypt, the geographical note locating the place, the well that Isaac would later dwell by, 24:62; 25:11, the memorial of God's grace, a known place in the land) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Wherefore the well was called Beerlahairoi; behold, it is between Kadesh and Bered
the naming of the well Beer-lahai-roi
the well of the living God who sees, commemorating Hagar's meeting with God (v.13)
a standing memorial of God's grace in the wilderness (24:62)

schools.summary: The birth of Ishmael: 'and Hagar bare Abram a son: and Abram called his son's name, which Hagar bare, Ishmael' — Hagar (having returned and submitted, v.9) bearing Abram a son, and Abram naming him Ishmael (as the Angel had directed, v.11, the birth of Ishmael, Abram's first son, the naming according to God's word); Hagar bearing the son foretold (v.11, Abram, at last, having a son, though not the son of promise, the birth of Ishmael, the fruit of the expedient, v.2); 'Abram called his son's name... Ishmael' (Abram naming the child Ishmael, 'God hears,' v.11, in obedience to the Angel's word, Hagar must have told Abram of the Angel's appearance and the name, the naming according to the divine direction, the son named for God's hearing of Hagar's affliction) — Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: And Hagar bare Abram a son: and Abram called his son's name, which Hagar bare, Ishmael
the birth and naming of Ishmael
Hagar bearing the foretold son, Abram's first son
the naming according to the Angel's word (v.11)

schools.summary: Abram's age at Ishmael's birth: 'and Abram was fourscore and six years old, when Hagar bare Ishmael to Abram' — Abram eighty-six at Ishmael's birth (the date noted, marking the time of the birth, Abram having been eleven years in Canaan, entering at 75, 12:4, the dating of Ishmael's birth, thirteen years before the covenant of circumcision, 17:1, 25, when Abram was 99 and Ishmael 13); Abram's age (86) at Ishmael's birth fixing the chronology (the long wait, eleven years in Canaan, before even this son, and the further wait, to Abram's 100th year, 21:5, before Isaac, the son of promise); the noting of Abram's age (86) marking the interval before the next great event — the covenant of circumcision and the promise of Isaac (ch. 17), thirteen years later (Abram 99), the chronology of the patriarch's life, the long trial of faith between the promise and its fulfillment in Isaac) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And Abram was fourscore and six years old, when Hagar bare Ishmael to Abram
Abram's age at Ishmael's birth
the dated birth, eleven years into the sojourn in Canaan
thirteen years before the covenant of circumcision and the promise of Isaac (ch. 17)
